name: Unprotected Admin / Sensitive Functionality
description: Admin or sensitive endpoints are accessible to low-privilege or unauthenticated users because the server relies on URL obscurity instead of server-side authorization checks.
indicators: robots.txt or sitemap.xml reveals admin/management paths
JavaScript bundles expose hidden URLs (adminPanelTag, isAdmin checks)
Endpoints /admin, /management, /console, /dashboard respond 2xx to non-admin users
Response headers reveal framework with default admin panel
technique: 1. Recon: parse robots.txt, sitemap.xml, JS bundles for hidden paths
2. Access admin paths directly with a low-privilege or anonymous session
3. If redirected, capture the response body before the redirect (may leak content)
4. Try path variations: /Admin, /ADMIN, /admin/, /admin/dashboard
5. Check JS source for variables: isAdmin, adminPanel, role, privilege
success_condition: Low-privilege or anonymous actor receives 2xx on an admin endpoint and the response body contains privileged data (user list, config, secrets).

name: Parameter-Based Access Control (Privilege Escalation)
description: The server trusts a client-supplied role/privilege parameter rather than validating against the authenticated session, allowing an attacker to elevate their role.
indicators: URL parameters encode role/admin: ?role=user, ?admin=false, ?access=1
Cookie contains role indicator: isAdmin=false, role=user, access_level=1
Request body JSON includes role/privilege/admin field
Response reveals current role in body or cookie
technique: 1. Log in with a low-privilege account; capture the full request/response
2. Locate role parameters in: URL params, cookies, request body, hidden fields
3. Modify values: role=admin, isAdmin=true, access_level=99, admin=1
4. Replay the modified request
5. Confirm the response exposes admin functionality or sensitive data
variations: Switch content-type and send JSON body instead of form data
Tamper JWT payload when signature is not verified (alg:none)
success_condition: After modifying the role parameter the response grants elevated access or returns data only available to admins.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
description: An authenticated but low-privilege actor can access or modify another user's resources by substituting a predictable identifier (user ID, order ID, document ID) in the request.
indicators: URLs or request bodies contain numeric/GUID object IDs: /api/orders/1234, userId=5
Endpoint accepts IDs as path params: /api/users/{id}, /api/orders/{orderId}
ID values appear to be sequential or predictable
Authenticated endpoints return objects with owner fields (userId, ownerId, email)
technique: 1. Establish two sessions: user_a (attacker) and user_b (victim)
2. With user_b, create or identify a resource and note its ID
3. With user_a, call the endpoint substituting user_b's resource ID
4. If the response returns user_b's data → IDOR confirmed
5. Test write operations: PUT/PATCH/DELETE on user_b's resource with user_a's session
variations: Encoded IDs: base64, hash — decode to find sequential patterns
Nested object references: /api/users/1/orders/2 — substitute both IDs
POST body IDOR: submit JSON with another user's userId
Mass assignment: include id field in PUT body to reassign ownership
success_condition: Attacker actor receives 2xx response containing victim's private data (owner field matches victim ID, not attacker ID).

name: HTTP Method Override
description: The server respects X-HTTP-Method-Override or _method parameter, allowing an attacker to send a destructive verb (DELETE, PUT) disguised as a safe one (GET, POST).
indicators: Server accepts X-HTTP-Method-Override or X-Method-Override headers
POST endpoints accept _method URL parameter
API framework docs mention method tunnelling support
technique: 1. Identify a resource endpoint that responds to DELETE or PUT
2. Send a POST request with header X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE
3. Alternatively add query param: ?_method=DELETE
4. Check whether the resource was deleted or modified
success_condition: Server executes the overridden method and the response confirms the action (204, deleted resource, modified state).

name: Function-Level Access Control (Horizontal Privilege Escalation)
description: A user can invoke functions or endpoints that are only meant for users with the same role but different accounts — accessing another account's data or performing actions on their behalf.
indicators: Bulk user management endpoints: /api/admin/users
Account settings reachable by other users via predictable path
User IDs exposed in responses that belong to other accounts
technique: 1. Map all endpoints exposed to authenticated users
2. For each endpoint that takes a user/account identifier, substitute another user's ID
3. Test modification endpoints: change email, password reset, profile update
4. Look for bulk-data endpoints that return multiple users' data
success_condition: Actor can read or modify a different user's account data without authorization.

name: Forced Browsing / Missing Function-Level Access Control
description: Pages or API routes for privileged functions exist but are not linked to in the UI for low-privilege users. The server does not enforce authorization on these routes.
indicators: JavaScript reveals conditional UI for admin users (feature flags, role checks)
Guessable paths: /api/admin/*, /api/internal/*, /api/v2/debug/*
Directory listing exposed
Swagger/OpenAPI spec reveals endpoints not shown in UI
technique: 1. Extract all paths from JS bundles, Swagger spec, robots.txt
2. Attempt access with a low-privilege session to each discovered path
3. Record status codes and response bodies for unexpected 2xx responses
success_condition: Low-privilege actor reaches a function-level admin endpoint and receives a useful response (200 with data or 200 with admin UI fragment).
